w:r.w:t: Business Capability Model - Personas - Value Streams - Module Scope Catalog

Vol. 2 Application Architecture; Vol. 3 Technical Architecture; Vol. 4 Infrastructure; Vol. 5 Security Architecture; Vol. 6 Data Architecture; Vol. 7 Integration Architecture; Vol. 8 Deployment & DevOps; Vol. 9 Testing & QA

This volume is the Business Architecture layer of a nine-volume Enterprise Architecture Blueprint for a cloud accounting and business-management platform positioned as a functional peer to Zoho Books. It establishes the business capability model, stakeholder personas, value streams, and scoped module catalog that every downstream artifact - the BRD, SRS, FRS, Database Design Document, UI/UX Specification, API Design Document, Security Architecture, Testing & QA Specification, and Deployment & DevOps Guide - must trace back to.

Enterprise architecture programs at this scale fail most often not from a lack of diagrams but from an absence of a shared, unambiguous definition of "what the business does" before anyone designs "how the system does it." This document exists to remove that ambiguity. Every capability, persona, and value stream defined here becomes a first-class, numbered reference (e.g. BC-04.2, VS-03, PER-06) that later volumes cite directly, so a requirement in the SRS or a table in the DDD can always be traced back to a business reason for existing.

The full blueprint is being produced in phases, one architectural domain at a time, with review and sign-off between phases so each subsequent volume builds on agreed, stable foundations rather than assumptions. The nine volumes and their dependency order are:
Application Architecture (module boundaries, DDD, C4 diagrams)
Technical Architecture (stack, coding standards, folder structure)
Data Architecture (ERD, schema, multi-tenancy data model)
Security Architecture (threat model, IAM, compliance controls)
UI/UX Design Specification & API Design Document
Can start in parallel with Vol. 3

The BRD, SRS, and FRS are intentionally not listed as separate volumes: in this blueprint their content is absorbed into Volumes 1-2 (business and functional scope) and Volume 9 (interface-level detail), which avoids the duplication that typically causes an SRS and an architecture document to drift out of sync in real enterprise programs. If a standalone SRS/FRS/BRD deliverable is still required for procurement or compliance reasons, Section 12 of this document shows exactly how to extract one from the numbered items here.
Section 4 (Business Capability Model) is the master index - every other section refers back to a capability ID such as BC-07.
Section 6 (Module & Feature Scope Catalog) is the working scope document - treat it as the object of change control going forward.

Section 7-8 (Value Streams & Process Flows) contain Mermaid diagram source blocks. They are intentionally kept as editable text rather than static images so they can be pasted directly into any Mermaid-compatible renderer, version-controlled, or regenerated as the design evolves.
Section 11 (Traceability Matrix) is what Vol. 2 onward will cite - do not renumber capability IDs after this document is approved without a corresponding renumber pass across all volumes.

Deliver a cloud-native, multi-tenant accounting and business-operations platform that lets small and mid-sized businesses run receivables, payables, banking, inventory, projects, and compliance from one system of record - matching the functional depth of Zoho Books while being architected, from day one, for API-first extensibility and horizontal scale.

The product is scoped against three incumbents whose feature sets define the addressable functional baseline: Zoho Books (primary reference - see Section 5 module catalog, derived from its published feature set as of July 2026), QuickBooks Online, and Xero. The competitive intent is functional parity on core accounting, receivables, payables, banking, and inventory within the first two release phases, with AI-assisted workflows (Section 5, BC-18) and open API/integration architecture (Volume 5) as differentiation vectors rather than table-stakes features.
Fast invoicing, expense capture, tax-ready books
Reach functional parity with Zoho Books core modules
100% of BC-01 through BC-13 (Section 4) shipped and passing UAT
Multi-tenant SaaS from day one, not retrofitted
Tenant isolation verified under load; zero cross-tenant data leakage in security audit
API-first so every UI action has an equivalent API call
100% of write operations exposed via documented REST/API endpoints
Compliance-ready for US, EU, and India tax regimes at launch
Sales tax (US), GST (India), VAT (EU) engines pass regulator-aligned test suites
Sub-second core transaction response at 10,000 concurrent tenants
P95 latency benchmarks defined in Vol. 3/7 met under load test

These principles are binding constraints on every downstream volume. Where a later architectural decision conflicts with one of these, the conflict must be raised and resolved explicitly (an ADR in Volume 2 or 3), not silently overridden.
Multi-tenancy is a first-class concern, not an afterthought
Every table, cache key, queue message, and log line must carry a tenant identifier from the first schema draft (Vol. 4).
Every business capability must be independently testable and independently deployable in the medium term
Application Architecture (Vol. 2) must define bounded contexts with explicit contracts, not a single monolithic module.
The general ledger is the single source of financial truth
All modules (invoicing, bills, payroll, inventory valuation) post to a canonical journal entry model - no module maintains a shadow ledger.
Every financial mutation is append-only at the ledger level; corrections are reversing entries, never in-place edits (Vol. 4, Vol. 6).
API and UI are equal citizens
No business operation may be UI-only; the UI is a client of the same API surface third parties use (Vol. 9).
Compliance and locale rules are configuration, not code branches
Tax engines, number formats, and statutory reports are driven by tenant/country configuration (Vol. 5).
Prefer custom fields, workflow rules, and webhooks (BC-16, BC-19) over per-tenant code forks.

Personas drive both the module priority (Section 2.3) and the permission model that Volume 6 (Security Architecture) will formalize into roles. Each persona is tagged with the value streams (Section 7) it participates in.
Overall visibility, approvals, org & user setup, editions/billing
Read/adjust access to a client's books via Accountant Module
Quotes, invoices, payment collection, customer communication
Purchase orders, bill entry, vendor payments
Stock levels, adjustments, reorder points, batches
View/pay invoices, accept quotes, message vendor via portal
Submit invoices, view PO/payment status via portal
Roles, security, integrations, audit trail review

The capability model is organized in two levels: Level-1 capability domains (BC-01 through BC-21) and Level-2 sub-capabilities. This is the canonical numbering that Volume 2 (Application Architecture) will map 1:1 onto bounded contexts / services, and that Volume 4 (Data Architecture) will map onto schema boundaries.
Chart of accounts; journal entries; multi-currency posting; base-currency revaluation; transaction locking; budgets
Quotes; sales orders; invoices (incl. recurring, progress, retention); credit notes; revenue recognition; sales approvals
Purchase orders; vendor bills; vendor credits; 3-way match (BillPay); purchase approvals
Expense capture; receipt OCR/auto-scan; recurring expenses; categorization; mileage
Bank feed connections; auto-categorization / bank rules; statement import; reconciliation
Payment links; card/ACH processing; auto-charge on recurring invoices; payment gateway integrations
Item master; price lists; reorder points; adjustments; composite items & item groups; packages/shipments; batch tracking (FIFO/FEFO/LIFO); unit groups
Asset register; depreciation schedules; disposals; asset lifecycle reporting
Project budgets; timers; timesheet approvals; project billing; profitability reporting
Pay runs; statutory deductions; payslips; payroll-to-ledger posting
Sales tax / VAT / GST engines; tax exemptions; 1099/W-9 management; e-filing integrations
Currency exchange handling; 25+ language localization; locale-aware number/date formats
70+ standard reports; custom report builder; reporting tags/divisions; scheduled report delivery; BI integration
Central document store; attachments to transactions; folder organization
Customer portal; vendor portal; accountant module; internal comments/tasks; voice/video/screen-share
Recurring transaction automation; approval workflows; trigger/notify/validate rule engine ("Blueprints")
Custom fields; custom modules; custom templates; layout/conditional field rules; custom report permissions
Conversational query assistant; anomaly detection; proactive insights; agent/MCP connectivity
Payment gateways; e-commerce channel sync (Shopify, Amazon, Walmart, etc.); native app ecosystem; public API & webhooks
User roles & custom roles; audit trail; HIPAA/PCI/GDPR controls; SSO
Multi-org / multi-tenant administration; editions & entitlements; multi-device access (web, mobile, desktop)

This section resolves each capability domain into a release-scoped feature list. "MVP" = required for the first production release to be usable and credible against Zoho Books' Standard edition. "R2" = second release, aligned to Zoho Books' Professional-tier depth. "R3" = third release / Enterprise-tier depth. This table is the primary object of change control - any scope change should be proposed as an edit to this table with a rationale, not decided ad hoc in a later volume.
Item master, stock levels, reorder points
Pay runs, statutory filing (phased by country)
Core financial statements (P&L, balance sheet, cash flow)
Vendor portal, accountant module, team collaboration
Single org per tenant, web app

A value stream is the end-to-end sequence of activities that delivers value to a persona, cutting across multiple capability domains. Volume 2 will decompose each value stream into service interactions; this section fixes the business-level shape so that decomposition has a stable target.
Persona: PER-04 (Sales Clerk), PER-09 (Customer), PER-06 (Inventory Manager). Capabilities touched: BC-02, BC-05, BC-06, BC-07, BC-15.
B -- Yes --> C[Convert to Sales Order]
G --> H[Customer Pays Online or Offline]
Persona: PER-05 (Purchasing Clerk), PER-10 (Vendor), PER-06 (Inventory Manager). Capabilities touched: BC-03, BC-05, BC-07, BC-15.
A[Raise Purchase Order] --> B[Vendor Confirms / Ships]
C --> D[Vendor Submits Bill via Portal]
D --> E{3-Way Match: PO vs Receipt vs Bill}
Persona: PER-02 (Bookkeeper), PER-03 (External Accountant), PER-01 (Owner). Capabilities touched: BC-01, BC-05, BC-13, BC-20.
A[Daily Transactions Post to Ledger] --> B[Bank Feed Reconciliation]
Persona: PER-07 (Project Manager). Capabilities touched: BC-09, BC-02, BC-04.
A[Set Up Project Budget] --> B[Team Logs Timesheets]
C -- Approved --> D[Bill Client: Hours / Fixed / Milestone]
E --> F[Track Profitability: Budget vs Actual]
Persona: PER-08 (Payroll Admin), PER-01 (Owner). Capabilities touched: BC-10, BC-01, BC-11. (Release 3 scope - see Section 6.)
E --> F[Post Payroll Journal to Ledger]
Persona: PER-02, PER-03. Capabilities touched: BC-01, BC-05, BC-08, BC-13, BC-20.
Sys-->>Acc: Notify books ready for review
Acc->>Sys: Review journals & adjust if needed

Where Section 7 shows the end-to-end value stream, this section drills into the three highest-risk processes at decision-branch level of detail - these are the flows Volume 2 must decompose into service contracts first, since errors here have direct financial-integrity consequences.
Draft --> PendingApproval: submit (if approval rule active)
Sent --> Overdue: due date passed, unpaid
PO[Purchase Order] --> Match{Match PO, Receipt, Bill?}
Match -- Quantities & prices match --> AutoApprove[Auto-approve for Payment]
Match -- Quantity mismatch --> QtyFlag[Flag: Quantity Variance]
Match -- Price mismatch --> PriceFlag[Flag: Price Variance]
QtyFlag --> ManualReview[Route to AP Clerk for Review]
Feed[Bank Feed / Statement Import] --> Raw[Raw Bank Transactions]
UserConfirm -- Yes --> Matched[Matched to Ledger Transaction]
UserConfirm -- No, create new --> NewTxn[Create New Ledger Transaction]

Mirroring the Zoho Books tiering model (Standard / Professional / Enterprise), editions are an entitlement layer over the capability model in Section 5 - not a separate codebase. Volume 3 will define the entitlement-check mechanism (feature flags evaluated per tenant).
Multi-country VAT/GST engines with e-filing integrations
GDPR data subject rights tooling, data residency options
PCI SAQ-D alignment if card data is ever touched directly
Not applicable (no PHI in accounting data by default)
HIPAA-aligned controls only if a healthcare-vertical edition is pursued
Append-only change log on financial entities
Full tamper-evident audit trail with drill-down UI
11. Traceability Matrix - Business to Downstream Volumes

This table is the contract between this document and everything that follows. Every capability ID must appear in Volume 2's bounded-context map and Volume 4's schema domain map before either of those volumes can be considered complete.
Bounded contexts / services, module dependency diagram, C4 containers
Schema domains, ER diagrams, tenant partitioning strategy
Feature-level user stories and acceptance criteria (FRS equivalent)
Service-to-service sequence diagrams per value stream
State machines as enum/status columns; transaction integrity rules
Non-negotiable constraints checked at each volume's review gate
12. Note on BRD / SRS / FRS Extraction

If a standalone Business Requirements Document, Software Requirements Specification, or Functional Requirements Specification is required (for example, for a vendor RFP, investor due diligence, or a compliance filing), it can be generated directly from this blueprint without re-deriving scope:
Sections 4 (Personas), 5 (Capability Model), 6 (Module Scope) - reformatted as shall-statements
Sections 6, 7, 8 - each Mermaid flow and scope row becomes a numbered functional requirement with acceptance criteria

This is intentional: producing them as extractions rather than independently authored documents guarantees they cannot drift from the architecture, which is the single most common documentation failure in enterprise programs of this size.
Target initial markets are US, India, and EU (drives BC-11/BC-12 scope) - confirm or adjust before Vol. 5.
Payroll (BC-10) is scoped as Release 3 given its heavy per-country statutory complexity - confirm this is acceptable given business timeline.

"Enterprise-class" is interpreted as multi-tenant SaaS with per-tenant data isolation, not single-tenant on-prem deployments. If on-prem/self-hosted is required, Vol. 4 and Vol. 7 scope changes substantially.
AI features (BC-18) are scoped as a differentiator, not MVP-blocking - confirm priority relative to core accounting parity.
Which countries must be supported for tax compliance at MVP vs. later releases?
Is a native mobile app required at MVP, or is a responsive web app sufficient for Release 1?
Should the accountant/bookkeeper multi-client experience (PER-03) be a Release 1 requirement, given it materially affects the tenant/org data model in Vol. 4?
What is the expected initial tenant scale (100s vs. 10,000s of organizations) - this directly sizes the infrastructure targets in Vol. 7?
Is white-labeling / reseller packaging in scope for any edition?

On approval of this volume, Phase 2 begins: Application Architecture (Volume 2), which will take the 21 capability domains from Section 5 and produce the module dependency diagram, C4 context/container/component diagrams, domain-driven design bounded contexts, and UML class/sequence diagrams needed to hand off to database and API design. Please review Sections 4-8 in particular, since capability IDs and value-stream names defined there become permanent references from this point forward.
